sessions.title: Kickoff
sessions.set_the_stage:
Welcome. Look around this room. This is your cohort. The people sitting next to you are the ones you will check in with, demo to, and stand alongside on Demo Day.

Most workshops end the moment the room empties. This one starts there. The weekend is the launch pad. The four weeks after are where the deeper builds happen, on your own time, with weekly office hours to keep you moving. Demo Day is where you stand in front of the room and show what you shipped.

Thirty minutes to set the expectation, then we start building.

questions.title: What do you want to walk out with on Demo Day?
questions.body: Not a vague aspiration. A specific sentence. By Demo Day I will have a portfolio that demonstrates I can _______. Write it down. Keep it visible all six weeks.

questions.title: What is the one thing that could stop you?
questions.body: Be honest. Time pressure during exams? Confidence? A tool you have never touched? Knowing the obstacle now lets you ask for help before it blocks you.

build.title: The program shape
build.instructions: Two days in this room. Then Week 1 chatbot, Weeks 2 and 3 agent, Week 4 Demo Day prep. Office hours every Saturday, 90 minutes, this room or on Meet. WhatsApp group for between-session unblocks. Demo Day in Week 5.

build.title: The portfolio at the end
build.instructions: Six builds plus a Demo Day recording, all collected at one URL. AI app, website, marketing collaterals (audio, video, brochure), chatbot, automation agent. All live. All yours. The URL is what you share with recruiters, on LinkedIn, and during placements.

build.title: How the room runs
build.instructions: When you are stuck for more than fifteen minutes, raise your hand. Do not power through silently. The facilitator walks the room precisely to catch the silent stuck. The fastest way to slow yourself down is to refuse help.

sessions.title: Pick your idea
sessions.set_the_stage:
Ninety minutes to land on the one micro-venture you will build over the next six weeks.

Eight ideas are on the screen. Each one is a real micro-venture that you, a family member, or someone you know could actually run. You pick one. Then you make it yours.

By the end of this session you have one sheet of paper. Your Ideation sheet. WHO you are building for, WHAT their problem is, and WHAT your build will do for them. The Ideation sheet is the first thing on your portfolio.

questions.title: Which of the ideas on the screen pulls you in?
questions.body: Read the set in front of you. If none of them pull you, switch to another set. Notice which one you keep coming back to. That is usually the right one. Trust the pull.

questions.title: Whose face do you see when you read it?
questions.body: If you picked Rent a Tree, whose farm is it? Yours? A relative's? If you picked Tiffin Choice, who is the cook? Write the name. The face makes the idea real. It might even be your own.

techniques: Is the person who would run this venture someone you know, or could it be you?
Can you talk to them today and ask one question? If it is a family venture, write the family member's name.
Is the problem something they actually face, or something you imagined?
When in doubt, message or call them now. One honest answer beats an hour of guessing.
Can you show this person what you built over the six weeks?
If you cannot reach them at all, pick a different person, or a different idea.

prompts.title: Sharpen your idea
prompts.content:
I am a college student in India. Over the next six weeks I am going to build a small, real venture for one real person, using AI tools to build all of it (a simple web app, a website, and so on). To be clear, this is not "an AI product." AI is just how I build it fast. The venture has to stand on its own by solving a real problem for a real person.

Pressure-test my venture idea like a sharp, honest mentor, not a cheerleader. Search the real web for evidence where you can, give sources, use rupees and real Indian examples, and say clearly when something is a guess.

My idea:
- The one specific person it is for (a real human, not "students" or "people"): [...]
- The exact moment the problem bites them: [...]
- What they do today instead: [...]
- What I will build for that person, in one plain sentence: [...]

Work through all six, specific to my exact person and to India:

1. IS THE PROBLEM REAL. Find evidence that people like this person actually face this problem (forum posts, articles, Reddit, news, anything real). Then give me 3 blunt questions I can ask my person that reveal real pain, about what they did last time, not whether they "would use an app."

2. WHO ALREADY SOLVES THIS. Search and name 3 to 5 real things solving this in India today (apps, websites, local services, or the plain manual workaround), with links. For each: what they get right, and the one gap my small local version could own. Be concrete.

3. CAN A STUDENT BUILD IT WITH AI IN SIX WEEKS. I will build a single-page web app with AI tools, no payment gateway, no live tracking, no app store. What is the smallest version that still solves the core problem? Tell me plainly what to build, what to fake or do by hand, and what to cut.

4. THE MONEY ON ONE CUSTOMER. For one customer, one use: what would they realistically pay, and what does it cost ME to deliver (materials, delivery, my own time) in rupees? If the maths does not work on one customer, say so. (Building it with AI is nearly free. I mean the real-world cost of running the venture.)

5. WHY NOW. What has changed that makes this doable for a student today and not three years ago? If "why now" is weak, tell me.

6. THE RISKIEST ASSUMPTION. The single belief that, if it is wrong, kills this venture. And the fastest way I could test it this week with real people, not by guessing.

End with a 3-line verdict: build it as-is, build it if I change one thing (which), or swap it for a sharper version of the same idea. Be honest. I would rather hear it now than on Demo Day.

build.title: Read the idea cards on the screen
build.instructions: Eight micro-ventures are on screen. Read each one carefully. Each card tells you who it is for, the problem, and what the app does. If none fit, switch sets using the pills at the top.

build.title: Pick one and make it yours
build.instructions: Pick the one that pulls you in. Then make it specific to your life. Rent a Tree becomes your family's farm. Tiffin Choice becomes the cook you actually know. The card is a starting point. You make it real.

build.title: Write your Ideation sheet
build.instructions: One sheet. Top: WHO (the name of the person who will run this venture, even if that is you). Below: WHAT THEY STRUGGLE WITH (one sentence). Below: WHAT MY BUILD WILL DO (three lines, plain English).

build.title: Share with the room
build.instructions: In the last few minutes, each founder reads their sheet aloud. Two sentences: who you are building for, and what you are building. The room listens.

sessions.title: Build your app
sessions.set_the_stage:
You have an idea and a user. Now you build the real thing.

Today you build a working product for your venture. Not a slide, not a mockup. A real page on the internet that shows what you offer, lets a customer place an order, emails you the moment it comes in, saves every order to a database, and gives you a dashboard to see them all.

You do not write the code. Claude does. You tell it about your venture in plain words, paste in your own details, and put the pieces together one layer at a time. Tiffin, plants, sarees, tutoring, it does not matter what you sell. The steps are the same.

We go in order: set up your tools, build the order page, add the email, save to the database, build the dashboard, test it on your laptop, then put it live. By the end you have a real web address you can send to a real customer.

Follow the steps on screen. The exact prompts to copy are on the prompts page on your laptop. When you get stuck, the fix-it prompt is there for you.

prompts.title: Build your order page
prompts.desc: Creates index.html: shows your offerings, lets a customer choose, and place an order.
prompts.content:
I am building a simple website for my small venture. It is not an AI product. I am just using you to write the code for me. I will host it as one plain HTML file, with no backend.

Here is my venture:
- What I sell: [one line, for example home tiffins, rented plants, handloom sarees, exam tutoring]
- Who buys it: [one line, my customer]
- My offerings, one per line as Name | photo link or leave blank | short description or details like nutrition, size, material | price in rupees:
  [Name] | [photo link or blank] | [description or details] | [price]
  [Name] | [photo link or blank] | [description or details] | [price]
  (add as many as you have)
- If my offerings are grouped, the grouping is: [by day, by collection, by plan, or none]

Build me one file called index.html that does this:
- Shows my venture name and a one line tagline at the top.
- Shows each offering as a card with its name, its photo (use the link if I gave one, otherwise a neat placeholder I can swap later), its description or details, and its price. Group them if I said so.
- Lets the customer add an offering to their order and choose a quantity.
- Shows a running list of what they picked and the total in rupees.
- Has a short form for the customer name and phone number, and an optional note.
- Has one clear button labelled Place order, not Submit.
- After they place the order, shows a friendly confirmation on the screen with their order summary.

Keep it clean and easy to read on a phone. Simple colours, nothing flashy. Put all my offerings near the top of the file so I can edit them easily later. No payment, no login, no backend yet. Do not add email or database steps yet, I will ask for those next. Give me the full index.html.

prompts.title: Add the email notification
prompts.desc: Wires EmailJS in so you get an email the moment an order comes in.
prompts.content:
Here is my current index.html:

[paste your full index.html]

I want an email to reach me every time a customer places an order. I am using EmailJS. From my EmailJS account I have:
- Public key: [paste]
- Service ID: [paste]
- Template ID: [paste]

My EmailJS template uses these fields: customer_name, customer_phone, order_details, order_total, customer_note.

Add EmailJS to my page so that when the customer taps Place order, it sends me an email with their name, phone, the list of items they ordered as readable text, the total, and their note. Load the EmailJS library from their CDN. Keep everything else working exactly as it is now. If the email ever fails, still show the customer their confirmation and log the error to the console. Give me the full updated index.html.

prompts.title: Save orders to your database
prompts.desc: Wires Supabase in so every order is saved and never lost.
prompts.content:
Here is my current index.html:

[paste your full index.html]

I also want every order saved to my database so I never lose one. I am using Supabase. I have:
- Project URL: [paste]
- Anon public key: [paste]
- A table called orders with these columns: customer_name (text), customer_phone (text), items (text), total (number), note (text). Supabase adds id and created_at on its own.

Add Supabase to my page using their JavaScript library from the CDN. When the customer taps Place order, save the order into the orders table at the same time as sending the email. Put all the items they picked into the items column as readable text. Keep the email and the on screen confirmation working. If saving ever fails, still show the confirmation and log the error to the console. Give me the full updated index.html.

prompts.title: Build your orders dashboard
prompts.desc: Creates dashboard.html: a private page where you see every order.
prompts.content:
Build me a second file called dashboard.html. This is a private page where I, the venture owner, can see all my orders.

It reads from my Supabase database. I have:
- Project URL: [paste]
- Anon public key: [paste]
- A table called orders with columns: id, created_at, customer_name, customer_phone, items, total, note.

The page should:
- Load all orders from the orders table, newest first.
- Show them in a clean list or table: when it came in, customer name, phone, what they ordered, the total, and the note.
- Show the number of orders and the total rupees at the top.
- Have a Refresh button to load the latest orders.
- Look clean and readable on a phone and a laptop.

No login for now, it is just for me. Use the Supabase JavaScript library from the CDN. Give me the full dashboard.html.

prompts.title: Fix it when something breaks
prompts.desc: Your go-to whenever a step does not work. Paste the error and your file.
prompts.content:
Something on my page is not working. Here is what is happening:

[describe exactly what you did, what you expected, and what actually happened. If there is red error text in the browser console, press F12 to open it and paste the error here.]

Here is my current file:

[paste the whole file]

Find the problem and give me back the full corrected file. Tell me in one or two plain lines what was wrong, so I understand it. Do not change anything that is already working.

output_capture.ask: Paste two links: your live order page, and your live dashboard.
output_capture.guidance: These are the Netlify addresses for your order page and your dashboard. Both should open on any phone with no login.

sections.name: Set up your tools
sections.note: You do this once. Get every account ready before you build. Take it slow here and the rest is easy.

steps.title: Set up VS Code
steps.body: This is where your files live and where you run your site on your own machine.
detail: Go to code.visualstudio.com and download VS Code for your computer. Install it like any normal app.
Open VS Code. On the left bar, click the Extensions icon, the four little squares.
Search for Live Server by Ritwick Dey and click Install. This lets you open your site in a browser with one click later.
Make one folder on your computer named after your venture. In VS Code choose File, then Open Folder, and pick it. All your files go here.

steps.title: Set up GitHub
steps.body: GitHub stores your code online and is how your site goes live. The desktop app means you never touch the command line.
detail: Go to github.com and create a free account with an email you check.
Go to desktop.github.com, download GitHub Desktop, install it, and sign in with that account.
That is all for now. You use it near the end to publish your site.

steps.title: Set up Netlify
steps.body: Netlify takes your code from GitHub and puts it on the internet, free, with its own web address.
detail: Go to netlify.com and click Sign up.
Choose Sign up with GitHub and approve it. This links the two so Netlify can see your project later.
Stop there. You connect your project at the Go live step.

steps.title: Set up EmailJS
steps.body: EmailJS emails you every time someone orders, straight from your page, with no server.
detail: Go to emailjs.com and create a free account.
Click Add New Service, choose Gmail or your email, and connect your inbox. Note the Service ID.

Click Email Templates and create a template. In the body use these fields: {{customer_name}}, {{customer_phone}}, {{order_details}}, {{order_total}}, {{customer_note}}. Save it and note the Template ID.
Open Account and find your Public Key.
Write down three things: Public Key, Service ID, Template ID. You paste them into Prompt 2.

steps.title: Set up Supabase
steps.body: Supabase is your free database. Every order is saved here, and you get a built in table view as a bonus.
detail: Go to supabase.com, sign up free, and click New project. Give it your venture name and a password you save. Wait about a minute.
On the left open Table Editor and click New table. Name it exactly orders. Turn off Enable Row Level Security for now to keep things simple.
Add these columns, besides the id and created_at it makes for you: customer_name (text), customer_phone (text), items (text), total (numeric), note (text). Save the table.
On the left open Project Settings, then API. Copy two things: the Project URL and the anon public key. You paste them into Prompts 3 and 4.

sections.name: Build the order app
sections.note: Now you build, one layer at a time, with Claude. Each prompt adds one piece.

steps.title: Run Prompt 1, build your order page
steps.body: Open Claude and run Prompt 1. Fill the brackets with your real venture and offerings. Claude gives you a full index.html.
detail: Open the prompts page on your laptop and copy Prompt 1.
Paste it into Claude. Replace every part in brackets with your own venture, your offerings, their prices, and details.
Claude writes a full index.html. Read what it made. If something is off, tell Claude in plain words and it will fix it.

steps.title: Put it in VS Code
steps.body: Get Claude's code into a real file on your computer.
detail: In VS Code choose File, then New File. Save it as index.html inside your venture folder.
Copy all of Claude's code, paste it into that file, and save. Ctrl S on Windows, Cmd S on Mac.

steps.title: Run Prompt 2, add the email
steps.body: Now make it email you on every order, using your EmailJS keys.
detail: Copy Prompt 2 from the prompts page.
Paste it into the same Claude chat. Paste your full index.html where it asks, and paste your three EmailJS values.
Claude returns the updated index.html. Copy it back into your VS Code file, replacing what was there, and save.

steps.title: Run Prompt 3, save to the database
steps.body: Now make every order save to Supabase as well.
detail: Copy Prompt 3 from the prompts page.
Paste it into the same Claude chat. Paste your current index.html and your Supabase Project URL and anon key.
Claude returns the updated index.html. Copy it back into your VS Code file, replacing what was there, and save.

sections.name: Build the dashboard
sections.note: A private page, just for you, to see your orders.

steps.title: Run Prompt 4, build your dashboard
steps.body: Claude builds a second page that reads your orders from Supabase.
detail: Copy Prompt 4 from the prompts page.
Paste it into Claude with your Supabase Project URL and anon key.
Claude writes a full dashboard.html.

steps.title: Put it in VS Code
steps.body: Save the dashboard next to your order page.
detail: In VS Code choose File, then New File. Save it as dashboard.html inside the same venture folder.
Paste Claude's dashboard code in and save.

sections.name: Test it on your own machine
sections.note: Find the problems here, on your laptop, before anyone else sees them.

steps.title: Open it with Live Server
steps.body: Run your site locally with one click.
detail: In VS Code right click index.html and choose Open with Live Server. Your order page opens in your browser.
This is your site running on your own machine. Nobody else can see it yet.

steps.title: Place a test order
steps.body: Use your own page the way a customer would.
detail: Pick an offering, set a quantity, fill in your own name and phone, and tap Place order.
You should see the confirmation on screen.

steps.title: Check all three landed
steps.body: One order should show up in three places.
detail: Your email: an order email should arrive in the inbox you connected to EmailJS. Check spam too.
Your database: open Supabase, Table Editor, the orders table. Your test order should be a new row.
Your dashboard: open dashboard.html with Live Server. Your order should appear in the list.

steps.title: Run Prompt 5 if anything is off
steps.body: Something not working? This is your fix-it prompt.
detail: If the page looks wrong, the email did not arrive, or the order did not save, do not panic.
Copy Prompt 5 from the prompts page. Tell Claude exactly what you did and what happened, paste any red error text from the browser console (press F12), and paste your file.
Claude returns a corrected file. Replace your VS Code file, save, and test again. Repeat until all three land.

sections.name: Put it live
sections.note: Now the whole world can use it.

steps.title: Publish with GitHub Desktop
steps.body: Send your folder to GitHub in a few clicks.
detail: Open GitHub Desktop. Choose File, then Add Local Repository, and pick your venture folder. If it asks, click to create a repository, then Create.
Your files show up as changes. At the bottom left type a short summary like first version and click Commit to main.
Click Publish repository at the top. Keep it private if you like. Your code is now on GitHub.

steps.title: Connect Netlify and go live
steps.body: Netlify turns your GitHub project into a live website.
detail: In Netlify click Add new site, then Import an existing project, then GitHub. Pick your venture repository.
Leave the build settings empty, this is a plain site, and click Deploy.
In under a minute Netlify gives you a live web address like yourname.netlify.app. That is your order page, live. Your dashboard is the same address with /dashboard.html on the end.
From now on, whenever you commit and push in GitHub Desktop, Netlify updates your live site on its own.

steps.title: Test the real thing on your phone
steps.body: Open the live site on your phone, as a customer.
detail: Open your netlify.app address on your phone and place a real order.
Ask a friend to open it on their phone and order too. This is a real customer using your real product.

steps.title: Confirm it all works live
steps.body: The same three checks, now in the real world.
detail: The order email arrived in your inbox.
The order is a new row in your Supabase orders table.
The order shows on your live dashboard at /dashboard.html.

steps.title: Declare success
steps.body: You built and launched a real working product, powered by AI.
detail: Your order page is live and takes real orders.
You get an email for every order, every order is saved, and you have a dashboard to see them all.
Save two links for your portfolio: your live order page, and your live dashboard. Paste them into the cohort form.

sessions.title: Plan the website + marketing collaterals
sessions.set_the_stage:
You ship the website tomorrow morning. Tonight, you plan it.

A solopreneur without a website is like a shopkeeper without a shopfront. Your website is a conversation with a stranger who has never heard of you. A conversation that has to answer three questions in thirty seconds. What is this? Is this for me? What do I do next?

The biggest reason websites take twice as long as they should is that founders try to write copy live, in the website builder, while staring at a blank page. We are not doing that. In this block you write all the copy in a doc. Tomorrow you just drop it in.

Three pages of website copy. One ninety-second video script. One one-page brochure. By the end of this session you walk out with five documents, ready to use tomorrow morning.

No Carrd tonight. No recording. No design. Just writing.

questions.title: What does your landing page say in thirty seconds?
questions.body: If a stranger gives you thirty seconds, what three things must they take away? Write those three sentences. They become your headline, your sub-headline, and your call to action.

questions.title: What is the one specific outcome you promise?
questions.body: Not 'helps you study better'. A specific, namable outcome. 'Three resume versions in five minutes that match the role you actually want.' 'A flashcard set generated from your chapter PDF in under a minute.' Specific outcomes pull people in. Vague benefits push them away.

questions.title: What goes on the about page?
questions.body: Why did you build this? Not the business reason. The personal one. What did you see in your own life, or in someone close to you, that made this feel worth six weeks of your time? People do not trust products. They trust the person behind the product. The about page is where they meet you.

questions.title: What goes on the how-it-works page?
questions.body: Three steps. The user does step one, the AI does step two, the user gets step three. Plain language, no AI jargon. If someone read this section and then tried your app, would their expectations match what actually happens?

questions.title: What does your ninety-second video say?
questions.body: Three beats. The problem you saw. The thing you built. What it does for someone. Ninety seconds, three beats, thirty seconds each. Write the script as if you were speaking to one person, not a crowd.

questions.title: What does your one-page brochure include?
questions.body: A brochure is read at arm's length, not studied. Big headline. Three short sections. One QR code or URL at the bottom. What are the three sections that earn their space on a single page?

techniques: Count 'you' versus 'I' or 'we'. 'You' should appear far more than 'I' or 'we'.
Would your target user read the headline and think 'this is written about me'?
Read the copy out loud as if speaking directly to your target user.
Does the website describe what the product actually does, not what you wish it did?
Does the how-it-works page describe what the AI does in plain language?
After reading, does the visitor have an accurate expectation of what will happen when they try the app?
After thirty seconds, can someone say what the product does and who it is for?
Does the website name a specific outcome, in words a real user would use?
Does the website help the visitor understand why this is better than what they currently do?

prompts.title: Draft the landing page copy
prompts.content:
I am writing the copy for the landing page of an AI product I just built. I want the copy first, in a doc. Tomorrow morning I will drop it into Carrd.

The product: [one sentence, what it does]
The user: [one sentence, who specifically uses it]
The outcome they get: [one sentence, the specific thing they walk away with]
The live URL of the AI app: [paste your Netlify URL]

Write me the copy for a single-page landing on Carrd with these sections.

Headline. Under ten words. Names the outcome. Sounds like a real person said it.
Sub-headline. One sentence. Names the specific user.
How it works. Three short steps. Plain language. What the user does and what they get back.
Why this is better than what they do today. Three sentences naming the current way and why it is not good enough.
Call to action. One button. Specific verb. Leads to the AI app URL.

Avoid the AI-website voice. No 'revolutionary', no 'powerful', no 'seamless', no 'leverage'. Write it like one person describing what they made, to one other person.

prompts.title: Draft the about page copy
prompts.content:
I am writing the about page for the same product. The visitor has read the landing page and now wants to know who built this and why.

The product: [paste from previous]
The user: [paste from previous]

Here is the personal story behind this product. [Tell Claude your real reason for picking this idea. Be honest. Was it a problem you watched a family member struggle with? A frustration you had? Something you noticed at college? Two or three sentences in your own voice.]

Write me the about page copy with these sections.

A heading that introduces me by name.
A paragraph about where I saw the problem. Specific, not abstract. The actual moment or person.
A paragraph about why I think AI is the right tool for this, in plain words.
A paragraph about what I want to do with this beyond just building it.

First person. My name. My voice. Not corporate. Not 'our mission is'. Just one person talking about why they made the thing.

prompts.title: Draft the how-it-works page copy
prompts.content:
I am writing the how-it-works page. This page is for visitors who liked the landing page and want to understand the mechanics before they try the app.

The product: [paste]
The user: [paste]
What the AI is actually doing: [the verb you committed to in the AI app session]
What the user puts in: [paste]
What the user gets out: [paste]

Write me the how-it-works copy with three sections.

Step 1. What the user does to start.
Step 2. What the AI does in the background.
Step 3. What the user gets and what they can do with it.

For each step, two or three sentences. Plain language. No AI jargon. If a parent or a relative read this, they should understand what is happening at every step. End with a button that says 'Try it now' and links to the AI app URL.

prompts.title: Draft the ninety-second video script
prompts.content:
I am recording a ninety-second founder video tomorrow morning. Screen recording of me using the product, with me narrating over it. I will use the script you write tonight to record cleanly tomorrow.

The product: [paste]
The user: [paste]
The outcome: [paste]

Write me a ninety-second script in three beats of thirty seconds each.

Beat 1 (seconds 0 to 30). The problem I saw. Be specific. A real situation, not abstract pain. End on the sentence that names what I built.

Beat 2 (seconds 30 to 60). What it does. I am showing my screen during this beat. Tell me what I am doing on screen and what I am saying out loud at each moment.

Beat 3 (seconds 60 to 90). Who this is for, and what they get. End with my AI app URL spoken aloud.

Write it in spoken English, not written English. Short sentences. Real pauses. No 'in conclusion' or 'in summary'. The way an Indian college student actually talks to a friend, not how textbooks sound.

Flag any line that will be hard to say in one take and suggest a smoother alternative.

prompts.title: Outline a one-page brochure
prompts.content:
I am generating a one-page brochure tomorrow morning. PDF. I will use Canva or a similar tool. I need the text content tonight so tomorrow I just drop it into a template.

The product: [paste]
The user: [paste]
The outcome: [paste]
The URL: [paste]

Give me the text for a one-page brochure, in the order it should appear, with each block labelled.

Hero line. Same as website headline, can be slightly different if it works better in print.
The problem. Three sentences.
The product. Three sentences.
How it works. Three bullets.
Who this is for. One sentence.
Call to action. One line with the URL.

Keep the total word count under 250 words. A brochure is read at arm's length, not studied.

build.title: Save five documents
build.instructions: Five Google Docs or Notion pages. Landing page copy. About page copy. How-it-works copy. Video script. Brochure copy. Title them clearly. You will need all five in your hand tomorrow morning.

build.title: Read everything out loud, once
build.instructions: Read the landing copy out loud. Read the about page out loud. Read the video script out loud. Whatever sounds awkward gets fixed tonight, not tomorrow.

build.title: Show one peer in the room
build.instructions: Find one person, swap your video scripts. Read theirs. Tell them the one sentence that does not yet sound like a real human saying it. Receive the same. Edit.

test_output.title: The five-docs test
test_output.description: Do you have five documents saved with landing copy, about copy, how-it-works copy, video script, brochure copy? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The thirty-second test
test_output.description: If a stranger read only your headline and sub-headline, would they know what your product does and who it is for? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The about-page test
test_output.description: Does your about page read like one specific person wrote it, not like a corporate 'about us' template? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The spoken test
test_output.description: Does your video script sound like spoken English, not written English? Read it once more if you are unsure. If yes, pass.

sessions.title: Launch the website
sessions.set_the_stage:
Three hours. You walked in with three pages of website copy written and ready. You walk out with a live three-page website at a real URL , landing page, about page, how-it-works page , all linked to each other, all looking like they belong together, with a working button that takes a visitor to your AI app.

This is Launch. The shopfront opens. The audio, the founder video, and the brochure all come next, in the Brand session after lunch. Right now, the priority is the website skeleton , three pages, copy in place, mobile-ready, AI app linked.

The facilitator walks the room continuously. If you finish your three pages early, do not race ahead to Brand. Polish what you have. Open it on a friend's phone. Read it aloud once more. The Brand session begins together, after lunch.

questions.title: Which page does the visitor see first?
questions.body: Most visitors will land on your landing page. That page has to do the most work in the least time. The about page and the how-it-works page are for visitors who already want to know more. Plan accordingly.

questions.title: Are the three pages clearly linked?
questions.body: A visitor on the landing page should be able to reach about and how-it-works in one click. A visitor on the about page should be able to come back to landing in one click. Test the navigation on a phone, not just a laptop.

questions.title: Does it look like one person built this?
questions.body: Same fonts. Same colours. Same voice. The three pages must feel like one site, not three separate sites stitched together. Walk through all three before you call yourself done.

techniques: Open your published Carrd site as if you have never seen it. Where do your eyes go first?
Read every word aloud. Cut anything that sounds like marketing.
Would a stranger know what to do within three seconds of landing?
Test the link from the website to the AI app. Does it open in a new tab and load?
Test page-to-page navigation. Landing to about. About to how-it-works. How-it-works back to landing.
Test on a phone. Do the buttons reach the thumb? Does the text reflow without horizontal scrolling?
Does each page earn its place? If the about page is empty, cut it. If how-it-works just repeats the landing page, rewrite it.
Does the website point the visitor toward trying the AI app?
Would a recruiter who landed on this take it seriously?

prompts.title: Website production helper
prompts.content:
I have my website copy in three docs: landing page, about page, how-it-works page. I am about to drop them into Carrd. Walk me through, in order, what to do. Create the account, pick a template that supports multiple pages, paste each page's copy in the right place, set up the navigation between the three pages, link the AI app button to my Netlify URL, publish.

Keep it tight. Tell me the click sequence, not the theory.

build.title: Hour 1, website skeleton
build.instructions: Carrd account. Template chosen that supports multiple pages. Landing, about, and how-it-works pages all created. Copy pasted into the right sections. Link to the AI app live. Site published to a yourname.carrd.co URL. Do not polish yet, just get it up.

build.title: Hour 2, navigation and pages talk to each other
build.instructions: Every page has a clear way to get to the other two. Landing → about. About → how-it-works. How-it-works → landing (and to the AI app button). Test on a phone.

build.title: Hour 3, polish all three pages
build.instructions: Whitespace. Fonts. Mobile preview. One photo of you on the about page. Make sure the three pages feel like they belong to each other. Test every link including page-to-page navigation. Test on a friend's phone if you can.

test_output.title: The three-page test
test_output.description: Does yourname.carrd.co have a landing page, an about page, and a how-it-works page, all linked to each other and all looking like they belong together? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The phone test
test_output.description: Does the site look good and work cleanly on a phone, with page-to-page navigation reachable by thumb? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The AI app link test
test_output.description: Does the AI app button on the landing page open your live Netlify app in a new tab? If yes, pass.

output_capture.ask: Paste the live URL of your three-page Carrd website.
output_capture.guidance: This is the published Carrd URL, not the editor URL. Confirm all three pages load on a phone and the AI app button works. Audio, video link, and brochure all come in the Brand session after lunch.

sessions.title: Brand with audio, video, and brochure
sessions.set_the_stage:
Three hours. The website is live. Now you give it a voice, a face, and something a visitor can carry away with them.

This is Brand. Three pieces. Sixty seconds of audio in your voice, generated from your script, embedded on the landing page right after the headline. Ninety seconds of founder video, screen-recorded with your voice over it, posted as a native video on LinkedIn. One one-page brochure, generated as a PDF, linked from your website for visitors who want to share it with a parent or relative.

All three exist for the same reason: trust. A website is a stranger reading words. Audio, video, and brochure turn that stranger into someone who has met you.

The facilitator walks the room continuously. Lunch is the first 30 minutes of this block if you have not eaten yet , take it now. Be done by 15:00. Interim demo at 15:00. Hard deadline.

questions.title: Where does the audio belong on the page?
questions.body: Audio is most powerful when it lands right after the headline. The visitor reads your headline, hears your voice say what the product is, and then continues scrolling. Less powerful at the bottom. Worst at the very top before any context. Place it right after the headline.

questions.title: What does your LinkedIn post caption say?
questions.body: The video lives on LinkedIn. The caption above the video is what most people read instead of watching. Two lines. The first names the problem. The second names what you built and where to try it. The video then earns the click for those who want more.

questions.title: Where does the brochure link belong?
questions.body: Not at the top. The brochure is for visitors who already trust you and want something to share with someone else (a parent, a relative, a teacher). Place the download link low on the page, near a 'tell someone you know' invitation.

questions.title: Do all three sound like the same person?
questions.body: Your website voice, your audio voice, your video voice. They should be the same person. If your audio sounds nothing like your website, the website voice was probably not yours to start with. Read all three out loud, in order, before you call them done.

techniques: Listen to the audio without watching the screen. Does it sound like a person, or like an announcement?
Watch the founder video with the sound off. Does the screen activity hold attention even without your voice?
Read the brochure at arm's length. Does the eye know where to land first, second, third?
Test the audio on a phone, not just a laptop. Does it autoplay or does the visitor have to tap?
Test the LinkedIn post in your feed. Does the video autoplay? Is the caption two lines visible without expanding?
Test the brochure download. Does the PDF open on a phone and a laptop both?
Does the whole presence feel like it was made by one person, not stitched together?
Does every asset point the visitor toward trying the AI app?
Would a parent who saw the brochure understand what their child built?

prompts.title: Audio production
prompts.content:
I want to generate a 60-second audio piece from this script.

[paste your video script, the first 60 seconds, or a tightened version]

Give me the ElevenLabs settings to use. Voice selection, stability, similarity, style. I want it to sound like a calm, real person and not a typical AI voice-over. Warm, slightly understated, and one notch slower than the default pace.

prompts.title: LinkedIn post caption
prompts.content:
I am about to post my ninety-second founder video on LinkedIn. The video is embedded as a native video, not a link. I need a two-line caption that earns the click for people who scroll past without watching.

The product: [paste]
The user: [paste]
The outcome: [paste]

Write me three caption options. Each option is exactly two lines. The first line names the problem in a way that someone in my LinkedIn network would recognise. The second line names what I built and includes the AI app URL.

No hashtags in the caption itself. Suggest three to five hashtags separately.

build.title: Hour 1, audio recorded and embedded
build.instructions: 60 seconds of audio generated in ElevenLabs, downloaded as MP3, embedded on the landing page right after the headline. Test on a phone.

build.title: Hour 2, founder video recorded and posted
build.instructions: Loom or QuickTime screen recording, you narrating live with the script in your peripheral vision. Aim for ninety seconds. Re-record up to three times if needed, but no more. Upload to LinkedIn as a native video post with the two-line caption. Pin the post to your profile. Copy the post URL , you will paste it on the site.

build.title: Hour 3, brochure to PDF
build.instructions: Canva, one-page template. Drop in the brochure copy. Export PDF. Upload the PDF to Drive, set link to anyone-with-link can view. Copy the link, add a 'Download brochure' button to the website low on the landing page.

build.title: Final smoke test
build.instructions: Open your website on a phone, on mobile data. Land on the home page. Listen to the audio. Click through to the about page. Read it. Click through to how-it-works. Read it. Click the AI app button. Use the app. Come back. Click the LinkedIn video link. Watch it. Download the brochure. The full visitor journey, in one walkthrough. Anything that does not work gets fixed before the interim demo.

test_output.title: The audio test
test_output.description: Does the audio play when you tap the play button on a phone, right after the headline on the landing page? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The video test
test_output.description: Is the founder video live on your LinkedIn, with a real two-line caption, with the post pinned to your profile, and with the post linked from your website? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The brochure test
test_output.description: Does the brochure download as a PDF from the website link, on both laptop and phone? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The full-journey test
test_output.description: Did you walk a friend through the full journey, all three pages, audio, video, brochure, AI app, on a phone, before the interim demo? If yes, pass.

output_capture.ask: Paste your LinkedIn founder video post URL.
output_capture.guidance: Audio and brochure live on your website (already captured in the Launch session). The founder video is the one new public URL from Brand , pinned to your LinkedIn profile and linked from your website.

sessions.title: Interim Demo Day
sessions.set_the_stage:
Stop building. Stop polishing. Stand up.

For the last day and a half you have been heads-down. Now the room shifts. Every person in this cohort has ninety seconds to show what they made.

This is not the real Demo Day. The real Demo Day is in four weeks, with the full portfolio, recorded, shareable, in front of guests. This is the interim demo. Peer to peer, no slides, no rehearsal. You stand up, open your site on the screen, walk the room through the user's journey in ninety seconds, and sit down. The room claps. The next person stands up.

This hour is the most important hour of the weekend. Not because of the demos themselves, but because of what watching the whole room demo does to your own bar. By the time the room finishes, you will have ideas for what to add over the next four weeks. Write them down as you watch.

build.title: The ninety-second demo format
build.instructions: Open your Carrd site on the projector. Three beats. Beat 1: who this is for and what they were doing before. Beat 2: open the AI app and run one real input, the room watches the output appear. Beat 3: one thing you learned building this that surprised you. Sit down. The room claps.

build.title: Watch and write
build.instructions: While others demo, keep a doc open. Whenever you see an idea you want to steal, a section structure, a phrasing, a moment in the AI output, write it down. You will look at this list during the four-week stretch.

build.title: Three demos get the room's vote
build.instructions: At the end of the hour, the room votes for three demos that stood out. One for clearest problem, one for tightest AI output, one for sharpest delivery. Not winners. Just signal.

test_output.title: The demo test
test_output.description: Did every person in the cohort demo in under ninety seconds? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The notebook test
test_output.description: Did you write down at least five ideas from other people's demos to apply to your own build over the next four weeks? If yes, pass.

sessions.title: The four-week plan
sessions.set_the_stage:
You walked in two days ago with an idea on a card. You leave with a live AI app, a three-page website, an audio piece, a founder video, and a brochure. Five real things. All live. All yours.

The next four weeks are where the harder builds happen. Chatbot. Agent. Then Demo Day. You will do most of this on your own, with two forms of support. A weekly ninety-minute office hours session, this room or on Meet, same time, every week. And a WhatsApp group for between-session unblocks.

Forty-five minutes to hand over the materials, walk through the four-week shape, set the norms for how we support you, and lock the Demo Day date on every calendar.

questions.title: What hour of the week is yours?
questions.body: Pick a specific hour, every week, that is your Just Build hour. Not 'whenever I find time'. A specific hour, that fits around your classes and your assignments. Put it in your phone calendar before you leave the room. The students who finish the four weeks are the ones who treated this hour as non-negotiable.

questions.title: Who is your accountability partner?
questions.body: Pair up with one person in the room. Once a week, on your Just Build hour, you text each other one update. What you finished, what you are stuck on. Two-sentence accountability. Pick now. Exchange numbers.

build.title: Hand-out, the chatbot PDF
build.instructions: Every student gets the chatbot build PDF on their email and on WhatsApp. Detailed, step-by-step, screenshots for every screen. The expectation is you complete the chatbot build in Week 1 and bring questions to the Week 1 office hours.

build.title: Hand-out, the agent PDF
build.instructions: Every student gets the agent build PDF on their email and on WhatsApp. This is the harder build. Two weeks. Bring questions to the Week 2 and Week 3 office hours.

build.title: Office hours, same room, every Saturday
build.instructions: This room (or on Meet), this time, every Saturday, for the next four weeks. Ninety minutes. Facilitator walks through common stuck points proactively. The rest is live Q&A. Treat it as a meeting, not a class. You bring your screen, your question, your unblock.

build.title: WhatsApp group, norms
build.instructions: Group created tonight. Post screenshots, not text descriptions. 'It is not working' with no screenshot gets ignored. Screenshot plus what you tried plus the error message gets a reply within a few hours. Office hours are for stuck-for-thirty-minutes problems. WhatsApp is for quick unblocks.

build.title: Demo Day date and shape
build.instructions: Demo Day is locked. Date is on the calendar invite the facilitator just shared. Guests are invited. Alumni, recruiters, family if you want. Each of you gets three minutes plus one minute Q&A. Hard cutoff. Demos are recorded. The recording becomes part of your portfolio.

test_output.title: The calendar test
test_output.description: Is your weekly Just Build hour in your calendar? Are the four office hours dates in your calendar? Is Demo Day in your calendar? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The partner test
test_output.description: Do you know one person's name and number in this room, who you have agreed to check in with weekly? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The materials test
test_output.description: Do you have the chatbot PDF, the agent PDF, and the WhatsApp invite link on your phone before you leave this room? If yes, pass.

sessions.title: Strengthen the Launch with a chatbot
sessions.set_the_stage:
Welcome back. Week 1.

The Launch is live. The website is up, the audio is embedded, the video is on LinkedIn, the brochure is downloadable. What is missing is the moment when a visitor has a question and you are not there to answer. The chatbot is what fills that gap. It strengthens the Launch by giving every visitor someone to talk to, every hour of every day.

By now everyone in this room has worked through the chatbot PDF. Some of you have a chatbot live on your Carrd site. Some of you are stuck. Some of you have not started. All three are normal at this point.

Ninety minutes. We open with a quick status check, then walk through the four places students most commonly get stuck on the chatbot build, then open the floor for live Q&A. The last fifteen minutes we close with a roster check. Every person in the room confirms, out loud, whether their chatbot is live or what specifically they need to land it by next Saturday.
agenda: First 10 min, status check. Go around the room. Each person in one sentence: chatbot live, in progress, not started, or specifically stuck
Next 20 min, walkthrough of the four common stuck points on the chatbot build
Next 45 min, live Q&A. Screens up. Bring your specific question
Last 15 min, roster check. Every person says: live, or specifically what is left

stuck_points.title: The system prompt is too short
stuck_points.resolution: A chatbot system prompt of three sentences produces a chatbot that says useful things three times before going generic. The PDF has the long-form template. Use it. If yours is short, expand it before doing anything else.

stuck_points.title: The chatbot has no scope
stuck_points.resolution: Without an explicit 'do not answer questions outside X' instruction, the chatbot answers anything and feels like generic ChatGPT. Add the scope sentence at the top of the system prompt.

stuck_points.title: The embed code does not show up on Carrd
stuck_points.resolution: Carrd's free tier blocks some script tags. Use the iframe embed option instead, not the script embed. The PDF has the exact iframe HTML.

stuck_points.title: The chatbot never points to the AI app
stuck_points.resolution: The chatbot exists to convert visitors into AI app users. Every long answer should end with a soft invitation back to the app. Add that instruction to the system prompt explicitly.

test_output.title: The live test
test_output.description: By next Saturday, is your chatbot embedded on your Carrd site, visible on a phone, and producing useful answers for three test questions? If yes, pass. If no, post a screenshot to WhatsApp before Wednesday.

sessions.live_qa_note: Open the floor for 40 to 45 minutes. Screens up. Specific questions only. If someone has a general 'I am stuck' question, ask them to share their screen and walk through what they tried.
sessions.homework_check: Every person, around the room, in one sentence: chatbot live by next Saturday, or specifically what is left.

output_capture.ask: Paste your website URL again, now with the chatbot embedded.
output_capture.guidance: The chatbot lives on your Carrd site as an embedded widget. Confirm it appears when you open the URL on your phone.

sessions.title: Office hours, agent, week 1
sessions.set_the_stage:
Week 2. The agent build is harder than the chatbot build. That is by design. You have two weeks to ship it, not one.

This week you should be roughly halfway through the agent PDF. Your Make.com account is set up, the template is cloned, the trigger from your Carrd form is wired, and you have run one test scenario end to end, even if the email it sends is still rough.

The four stuck points in the first half of the agent build are different from the second half. We walk through this week's four. Next week we walk through the back-half four.
agenda: First 10 min, status check. Each person: agent trigger wired, in progress, not started, or specifically stuck
Next 20 min, walkthrough of the four common stuck points in the first half of the agent build
Next 45 min, live Q&A. Screens up. Make.com scenarios open
Last 15 min, roster check. Every person says: trigger working, or specifically what is left

stuck_points.title: The Carrd form is not triggering the Make scenario
stuck_points.resolution: Make.com's webhook needs the exact URL pasted into Carrd's form-action field, not the form-redirect field. The PDF page 8 shows the exact place. Check there first.

stuck_points.title: The scenario runs but does not see the form fields
stuck_points.resolution: The webhook needs to be re-listened after you change the form. In Make, click 'Redetermine data structure' on the webhook module, then submit the form once. The fields will appear.

stuck_points.title: The free tier is hitting the operations limit
stuck_points.resolution: Free tier is 1,000 ops per month, more than enough. If you are hitting limits, you are probably running the same scenario in a loop. Check whether you accidentally connected the scenario's output back to its trigger.

stuck_points.title: The email module asks for a Gmail connection and the connection fails
stuck_points.resolution: Gmail OAuth requires the Google account to allow third-party access. The PDF has the settings link. Open it in the same browser where you are logged into Make.

test_output.title: The end-to-end test
test_output.description: By next Saturday, can you submit your Carrd form and have an email arrive in your inbox within thirty seconds? Content can be rough. The pipe must be live. If yes, pass.

sessions.live_qa_note: Open the floor for 40 to 45 minutes. Screens up, Make.com scenarios visible. Most agent debugging happens by looking at the scenario diagram together.
sessions.homework_check: Every person, around the room, in one sentence: trigger-to-email working by next Saturday, or specifically what is left.

sessions.title: Office hours, agent, week 2
sessions.set_the_stage:
Week 3. The agent should be live by next Saturday.

The back half of the agent build is where the agent goes from 'sending an email' to 'sending an email that feels written for this specific person'. The plumbing is done. The voice is what is left.

This week's stuck points are about quality, not connectivity. The email module works. The question is whether what it sends sounds like the same person who wrote the website copy and narrated the video, or like a no-reply notification from an internet company.
agenda: First 10 min, status check. Each person: pipe working and improving voice, pipe still broken, or specifically stuck on quality
Next 20 min, walkthrough of the four back-half stuck points on the agent build
Next 45 min, live Q&A. Bring your sample emails. The room critiques
Last 15 min, roster check. Every person says: agent live, or specifically what is left for Demo Day

stuck_points.title: The email reads like an auto-confirmation
stuck_points.resolution: Pull your website copy. Pull your video script. The agent's voice must match. Rewrite the email body so it sounds like a continuation of the same person, not a transactional ping. Lead with one specific reference to what the user just submitted.

stuck_points.title: The agent does not handle empty or weird inputs
stuck_points.resolution: Add a router module in Make. If the form's main field is empty or under N characters, route to a polite 'I need a bit more to work with' response. The PDF page 22 has the router config.

stuck_points.title: The agent fires twice for one submission
stuck_points.resolution: Carrd's default form behaviour submits and refreshes. If you have 'submit and stay' enabled, some browsers fire twice. Switch to redirect mode and point to a thank-you page in Carrd.

stuck_points.title: Three test runs is not enough
stuck_points.resolution: Run the full scenario ten times before Demo Day, with different realistic inputs. Run it with your name. Run it with a friend's name. Run it from a phone. Each run reveals something the previous nine did not. If anything fails, fix it now, not the morning of Demo Day.

test_output.title: The live agent test
test_output.description: By Demo Day, can you submit your Carrd form three times with different inputs and have three good emails arrive, each one sounding like a real person wrote it for that specific submission? If yes, pass.

sessions.live_qa_note: Less debugging this week, more critique. Have students paste sample agent emails into the projector. The room reads them, names what sounds off, suggests fixes.
sessions.homework_check: Every person, around the room: agent fully live for Demo Day, or specifically what is left. Anyone with more than one specific issue gets a one-to-one slot with the facilitator before Demo Day.

output_capture.ask: Save your Carrd form URL (where the agent listens).
output_capture.guidance: The agent lives inside Make.com but listens to your Carrd form. Your portfolio page will show this URL alongside a description of what the agent does. Your facilitator also captures a screenshot of your Make.com scenario for the portfolio.

sessions.title: Office hours, pitch deck
sessions.set_the_stage:
Last office hours. Demo Day is one week away.

This is the session where your micro-venture becomes a brand. You walked out of the weekend with an app, a website, and an audio-video. You spent three weeks adding a chatbot and an agent. Today the venture gets a name, a logo, and a pitch deck that ties it all together.

Four steps in ninety minutes. Pick your venture name. Design your logo. Answer six questions. Build the deck in Gamma. You walk out with the deck you will pitch on Demo Day.
agenda: First 15 min, pick a name for your venture. Three approaches. Five options. Pick the one that lands.
Next 20 min, design your logo in Canva. Pick a template, drop in your name, choose a colour.
Next 15 min, answer six questions in a Google Doc. These answers go straight into the deck prompt.
Last 40 min, paste the prompt into Gamma, get a six-slide deck, upload your logo, tweak slides.

stuck_points.title: Cannot decide on a name
stuck_points.resolution: Three approaches. Person name (your name + what you do): 'Padma's Tutoring', 'Karthik Curates'. Place name (where it lives): 'Indiranagar Tiffin', 'Bagru Block'. Feeling name (what it gives the customer): 'Fresh Friday', 'Slow Plants'. Write 5. Read aloud. Pick the one that makes you smile.

stuck_points.title: Logo looking generic
stuck_points.resolution: In Canva, filter logo templates by SIMPLE or MINIMAL. Avoid templates with too many elements. One symbol or one wordmark is enough. Pick a colour that matches your venture's feeling, not a colour from the template gallery.

stuck_points.title: Gamma deck looks corporate
stuck_points.resolution: In the Gamma prompt, ask explicitly for 'warm colours, big visuals, short text, the kind of deck a founder presents to early customers and friends, not a startup investor.' If the first generation looks corporate, regenerate with that line emphasised.

stuck_points.title: Six questions feel like homework
stuck_points.resolution: They are not homework. They are the deck. The Gamma prompt is a template that pours your six answers into a six-slide deck in one shot. Write each answer in two short lines. Done in fifteen minutes.

prompts.title: Step 1: Pick your venture name (15 min)
prompts.content:
Spend 15 minutes brainstorming a name for YOUR specific venture. Not the generic idea name (Tiffin Choice, Rent a Tree etc). A name only you would pick.

Three ways to find a name:

1. The PERSON name approach: Your name + what you do. 'Padma's Tiffin', 'Karthik Curates', 'Anjali Bakes'.

2. The PLACE name approach: Where it lives. 'BalconyBuds', 'Indiranagar Tiffin', 'Bagru Block'.

3. The FEELING name approach: What it gives the customer. 'Fresh Friday', 'Slow Plants', 'Steady Mornings'.

Write 5 options. Read them aloud. Pick the one that makes you smile. That is your venture name.

prompts.title: Step 2: Design your logo in Canva (20 min)
prompts.content:
Open Canva. Free account.

1. Search 'logo' in the Canva templates.
2. Filter by SIMPLE / MINIMAL designs. Avoid templates with too many elements.
3. Replace the placeholder name with YOUR venture name from Step 1.
4. Pick a colour that matches your venture's feeling. Warm orange for plants. Mustard yellow for food. Deep green for farms. Pink for events.
5. Download as PNG with transparent background.

Save it. You will use it in the deck now and on your website later this week.

prompts.title: Step 3: Answer six questions (15 min)
prompts.content:
Answer these six questions in 2 lines each. Write them in a Google Doc. These answers go straight into your deck prompt.

1. WHO ARE YOU? Your name, your college, your year.

2. WHAT IS YOUR VENTURE CALLED? The name from Step 1.

3. WHAT IS YOUR ONE-LINE TAGLINE? What your venture does in 7 words or less. Example: 'Rent a tree. Eat the fruit for a season.'

4. WHO IS YOUR CUSTOMER? The real person who paid (or said they would pay). Name them. Where they live. What they do.

5. WHAT PROBLEM DID YOU SOLVE FOR THEM? In one sentence.

6. WHAT IS ONE REAL VALIDATION INSIGHT? What is one thing a real user said or did that proved the venture works? Quote them if you can.

prompts.title: Step 4: Build the deck in Gamma (40 min)
prompts.content:
Open gamma.app. Free tier gives you 400 credits. One deck takes 40 credits. Plenty.

Paste this prompt into Gamma. Replace the bracketed bits with YOUR answers from Step 3.

---

Build me a 6-slide pitch deck for my college micro-venture. I am a final-year engineering student.

[ANSWER 1: WHO ARE YOU]

MY VENTURE IS CALLED: [ANSWER 2]

TAGLINE: [ANSWER 3]

MY CUSTOMER: [ANSWER 4]

PROBLEM I SOLVED: [ANSWER 5]

REAL VALIDATION INSIGHT: [ANSWER 6]

My app is live at: [YOUR APP URL]
My website is live at: [YOUR WEBSITE URL]

Make the deck warm, simple, and visual. Not corporate. Six slides:

1. TITLE: My name, my venture name, my tagline. Reserve space for me to upload my logo.
2. THE PROBLEM: Who has it, the real customer from above, one specific example.
3. THE INSIGHT: What real user behaviour or quote proved the venture works.
4. WHAT I BUILT: Show that I have an app, a website, a chatbot, and an agent. Add URLs.
5. HOW IT WORKS: Three simple steps the customer takes.
6. WHAT COULD HAPPEN NEXT: If 20 paying customers used this, what would change?

Use warm colours. Big visuals. Short text. The kind of deck a founder would present at Demo Day to early customers, friends, and faculty, not to a VC.

build.title: The four-step arc
build.instructions: 15 min name. 20 min logo. 15 min answering questions. 40 min building the deck in Gamma. Stay with the clock.

build.title: Upload your logo into Gamma
build.instructions: Once Gamma generates the deck, drag your logo PNG from Canva onto slide 1. Place it next to your venture name.

build.title: Tweak the slides Gamma generated
build.instructions: Gamma usually nails 4 of 6 slides on the first try. Read each slide aloud. Fix the two that feel off. Do not regenerate the whole deck.

build.title: Leave with one polish
build.instructions: By the end of the call, every founder has one specific thing to fix on the deck before Demo Day. Not three. One. Specific.

test_output.title: The deck-on-projector test
test_output.description: If you screen-shared your deck on the call today and walked through the six slides in under three minutes, you are pitch-ready for Demo Day.

sessions.live_qa_note: Most founders over-engineer the deck. Six slides. Short text. Big visuals. The Gamma defaults are usually right. Do not spend the last 20 minutes choosing fonts.
sessions.homework_check: Every founder leaves with: a Gamma deck URL with all six slides filled in, their venture logo placed on slide 1, and one specific thing they will polish before Demo Day.

output_capture.label: Your pitch deck
output_capture.description: Save the Gamma share URL. This is the deck you present on Demo Day next Saturday.

sessions.title: Demo Day
sessions.set_the_stage:
Look at this room. It looks different from the morning of Day 1. That is intentional.

Five weeks ago you walked in with an idea on a card. You leave tonight with six live builds, collected into one URL, and a three-minute recording of yourself standing in this room demonstrating what you made to a room of strangers who had no reason to be impressed.

That recording becomes the most valuable thing in your portfolio. Recruiters do not read resumes anymore. They watch ninety seconds of footage and decide.

Demos this evening. Three minutes plus one minute of Q&A. Hard cap. We record every one. By the end of tonight every one of you has a Demo Day recording on your portfolio URL.

questions.title: What is your one specific ask?
questions.body: Every demo ends with one specific ask of the room. Not 'thank you'. Something the room can do tonight. Try the URL. Forward it to one person. Connect on LinkedIn. Send it to their parent or relative if relevant. Write your ask down before you pitch.

build.title: The four-beat demo
build.instructions: Beat 1: The problem and the person. Thirty seconds. Beat 2: The product, live on screen, with a real input. Ninety seconds. Beat 3: The one thing a real user said that you did not expect. Thirty seconds. Beat 4: The specific ask. Thirty seconds.

build.title: The Q&A minute
build.instructions: One minute for questions from the room. If no one asks, the facilitator asks. If you do not know the answer, say so. 'I do not know yet, but here is how I would find out' is a stronger answer than a guess.

build.title: Record everything
build.instructions: Camera on a tripod at the back of the room. Every demo recorded. Files uploaded the same night. Demo Day recording lives at the top of every student's portfolio URL by midnight.

test_output.title: The cap test
test_output.description: Did every demo come in under three minutes? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The recording test
test_output.description: Is every demo recorded, with audio that can be heard, and the screen capture clearly showing the AI app run? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The ask test
test_output.description: Did every demo end with one specific ask? If yes, pass.

output_capture.ask: Paste the link to the published Demo Day recording on your portfolio.
output_capture.guidance: The recording lives at the top of your portfolio URL. It plays automatically when someone opens the page. The recording is the strongest thing in the portfolio. Recruiters watch the demo, then explore the rest.

sessions.title: Close, what comes next
sessions.set_the_stage:
Stop for a moment. Look at the URL on your phone. Six weeks ago this did not exist. Not the product, not the website, not the chatbot, not the audio, not the video, not the agent, not the Demo Day recording.

You came in with an idea on a card. You leave with seven live things, collected into one URL.

This last block is about what you do with that URL across the next twelve months. Placements. Off-campus drives. Internship applications. Higher studies essays. LinkedIn that actually pulls recruiters to you. Your portfolio is the proof you can already do AI work. Use it everywhere.

At the very end, for those who want it, we open one more door. Orange Caterpillar Ventures for builders who want to keep building. Selective.

questions.title: What is the one thing you will do tonight with this URL?
questions.body: Before you sleep. One specific action. Update your LinkedIn featured section. Update your resume. Send the URL to one senior. Pin it to your WhatsApp status. The portfolio is only as valuable as the number of people who see it.

questions.title: What is the one thing you will do this week?
questions.body: Within seven days. Three specific people you will send this URL to, by name. A senior in your dream company. A college alum on LinkedIn. A professor or mentor. Write the three names down now. The portfolio works when it travels.

questions.title: What is the one thing you will do this month?
questions.body: Within thirty days. Apply to one off-campus drive. Apply to one internship through Internshala or LinkedIn. Reach out to one alum on LinkedIn for a fifteen-minute conversation. Walk into your college placement cell and show them your portfolio. Pick one and put it on the calendar.

techniques: Your portfolio is a long-running asset. Update it every time you build something new.
Personal messages beat generic applications. One DM with your portfolio URL to a senior at your dream company outperforms ten cold emails to careers@ inboxes.
If anyone responds, even with one line, reply within twelve hours. Speed signals seriousness.
Put the portfolio URL on your LinkedIn featured section, your resume, your Naukri profile, your Internshala profile, and your college placement portal. Five places, today.
Every change to your portfolio should be traceable to feedback. Not your own taste.
When you feel the urge to rebuild before sending, ask. Am I rebuilding because it is wrong, or because I am uncomfortable showing something imperfect?
Today. LinkedIn post drafted. Three people you will message identified.
Within 48 hours. Post published. Messages sent.
Next month. Apply to one off-campus drive or one internship where your strongest build is the proof. Walk into your placement cell and show them.
Placement season. Open your portfolio in the interview. Run the ninety-second demo from your phone.

build.title: Update LinkedIn now
build.instructions: Featured section: pin your Demo Day recording. About section: one sentence ending in your portfolio URL. Skills: add 'AI product development' if it is not there. Five minutes. Do it before you leave the room.

build.title: Update your resume now
build.instructions: Add one line under 'projects' or 'portfolio'. 'Built and shipped six AI products as a solopreneur in the Orange Caterpillar Just Build cohort. Portfolio: yourname.justbuild.orangecaterpillar.com'. Five minutes.

build.title: Send three messages tonight
build.instructions: Three people, before you sleep. A senior at a company you would like to work for. An alum from your college on LinkedIn. A professor or mentor who has shown interest in your work. WhatsApp or DM. One line: 'Spent six weeks building this, thought you might want to see.' Plus the URL. Speed signals seriousness.

test_output.title: The LinkedIn test
test_output.description: Is your Demo Day recording pinned to your LinkedIn featured section before you leave this room? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The resume test
test_output.description: Is your portfolio URL on your resume, in the projects section, before you leave this room? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The three-messages test
test_output.description: Will you send the three messages before you sleep tonight? If yes, pass.

ideas.name: Rent a Tree
ideas.for_whom: You, if your family has a farm, or a relative who has one
ideas.problem: Small farmers earn 30% of retail; middlemen control distribution. Urban families pay premium for fruit but cannot find a trusted direct source.
ideas.what_app_does: Shows a catalogue of trees with photos and the farmer's story. Customer picks a tree, pays for the season, sees updates over months. Produce delivers when ready.

ideas.name: Tiffin Choice
ideas.for_whom: You, if you cook well and could feed 30 office-goers nearby
ideas.problem: Customers do not know the weekly menu in advance. The cook does not know who wants what until she has already cooked.
ideas.what_app_does: Shows the upcoming weekly menu. Lets regular customers pick three preferred dishes for next week. Sends preferences to the cook.

ideas.name: Mehendi Bookings
ideas.for_whom: You, if you do mehendi, or a friend who does it for events
ideas.problem: Brides spend a week messaging five artists on Instagram. The artist who responds slowly loses the booking.
ideas.what_app_does: Shows the artist's portfolio by style. Customers see pricing per hand or design, check availability, book directly. Booking arrives on WhatsApp.

ideas.name: Makeup Enquiry
ideas.for_whom: You, if you do makeup well, or a friend who freelances
ideas.problem: Bridal makeup is a 15K to 75K booking. Brides spend a week comparing artists on Instagram and never get structured information.
ideas.what_app_does: Portfolio organised by occasion. Brides answer a guided questionnaire (event, venue, looks, allergies). Complete enquiry arrives on the artist's WhatsApp.

ideas.name: Jewellery Curator
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have access to a goldsmith, lac-maker, or silver craftsman
ideas.problem: Indian buyers shifting from showrooms to online want curated pieces with stories, not generic catalogues.
ideas.what_app_does: Collection by occasion and style. Each piece has the maker's story, weight, and care instructions. Custom orders go to the goldsmith via a guided form.

ideas.name: Alteration Service
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have access to a tailor, or your mother sews well
ideas.problem: Working women buy clothes online and most need small alterations. Local tailors do not do pickup-and-delivery.
ideas.what_app_does: Customers browse pricing, book a pickup slot, courier the piece with reference photos. The tailor returns it altered in four days.

ideas.name: Exam Prep
ideas.for_whom: You, if you are strong in one school subject
ideas.problem: Group tuitions are generic. Online platforms like Vedantu cost 50K-2L and are too broad.
ideas.what_app_does: Coach's specialisation, a 12-week or 24-week program structure, recent students' results. Parents see the plan, book a trial class, subscribe monthly.

ideas.name: Senior Mornings
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have an elderly parent or grandparent looking for structure
ideas.problem: Active retirees face a vacuum after retirement. Existing wellness apps target younger users. Joint-family support has shrunk.
ideas.what_app_does: One short reading or video daily, one practical task, a gentle log of yesterday. Family can check if the senior opened it today. Content pre-written, rotates by date.

ideas.name: Neighbourhood Bakery
ideas.for_whom: You, if you bake well, or a relative who already bakes for friends
ideas.problem: Demand grows beyond what WhatsApp can handle. Custom orders for birthdays and festivals get lost in chat history.
ideas.what_app_does: Bake catalogue with photos and prices. Custom orders use a structured form. Confirmed orders go to the baker as a clean WhatsApp message.

ideas.name: Tutoring Slots
ideas.for_whom: You, if you tutor younger students (Class 5-10), or a friend who does
ideas.problem: Parents make many calls to book a tutor. The tutor wastes evenings confirming slots over WhatsApp.
ideas.what_app_does: Tutor's subjects, sample lesson topics, available time slots, recent students' progress. Parents pick a free trial slot. Booking confirmed in person.

ideas.name: Plant Catalogue
ideas.for_whom: You, if you grow plants at home, or a neighbour aunty who runs a nursery
ideas.problem: Apartment dwellers want plants but kill them because they pick wrong for their light conditions.
ideas.what_app_does: Available plants with photos, prices, care difficulty (easy / moderate / demanding). Customers pick by their light, order, pay. Plants delivered by the founder.

ideas.name: Direct Dairy
ideas.for_whom: You, if your family has a small dairy farm, or a relative does
ideas.problem: Urban families pay 80-120/L for branded milk that is industrial dairy in fancy packaging. The farmer sells to wholesalers for 40/L.
ideas.what_app_does: Shows the farm and the cows. Customers subscribe to weekly home delivery of milk, paneer, ghee. Delivery on a fixed weekly route the farmer optimises.

ideas.name: Handicraft Brand
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have visited one craft cluster (Channapatna, Moradabad, Molela)
ideas.problem: Urban Indians want Indian-aesthetic decor. FabIndia is sterile and IKEA does not get the language.
ideas.what_app_does: Curated decor pieces with stories about the cluster and the artisan. New pieces drop monthly. Customers order, founder ships.

ideas.name: Hyperlocal Wash
ideas.for_whom: You, if you can hire two boys and serve one apartment complex
ideas.problem: Urban Indians spend 1K-2.5K/month getting their car washed badly. Reliable hyperlocal service does not exist at apartment-complex scale.
ideas.what_app_does: Residents of one complex subscribe monthly. Shows the wash schedule for the week. After each wash, residents see 'your car is done' on WhatsApp.

ideas.name: Two-Wheeler Service
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have a relative who runs a bike or scooter repair shop
ideas.problem: Customers drop their bike at the service shop and have no clarity on what was done or what it cost.
ideas.what_app_does: Customers fill in what is wrong and how urgent. Mechanic logs work done with photos. Customers get a clean bill on WhatsApp before pickup.

ideas.name: Pre-Loved Kids
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have access to many parents in your building or college network
ideas.problem: Kids outgrow good clothes in three months. Parents spend 30K-80K/year on briefly-used clothes.
ideas.what_app_does: Curated pre-loved kids clothes by size and brand. Sellers list pieces, founder verifies and ships. Buyers order, founder takes a margin.

ideas.name: Pet Sitter
ideas.for_whom: You, if you love pets and have time on weekends
ideas.problem: Working couples with dogs and cats struggle to find someone trustworthy and nearby to walk or feed their pet.
ideas.what_app_does: Sitter's profile and availability for the week. Pet owners book slots and pay. After each visit, the sitter sends a photo update on WhatsApp.

ideas.name: Fest Photographer
ideas.for_whom: You, if you do photography as a hobby and own a decent camera
ideas.problem: College fest organisers hire expensive professionals or whoever they know. No reliable middle option for student photographers.
ideas.what_app_does: Photographer's portfolio, packages (raw photos, edited highlights, reels), weekend availability. Fest organisers see pricing upfront and book.

ideas.name: Saree Curator
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have taste in sarees and a wholesaler contact
ideas.problem: Working women want curated sarees but online shops show thousands of generic options. Instagram saree sellers are unreliable.
ideas.what_app_does: Curated collection by region, occasion, or weave. Each saree has the weaver's story, fabric details, styling suggestions. Customers order, founder ships.

ideas.name: Block Print Brand
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have visited Bagru, Sanganer, or another block-printing cluster
ideas.problem: Urban buyers want authentic block prints but FabIndia is corporate and Instagram resellers are unreliable.
ideas.what_app_does: Curated collection of block-printed clothes and home goods. Each piece tells the story of the cluster and the printer. New drops monthly.

ideas.name: Home Pickle
ideas.for_whom: You, if your mother or grandmother makes legendary pickles
ideas.problem: Industrial pickles have killed the taste people remember from home. Urban Indians cannot find a small home-maker they trust.
ideas.what_app_does: Product range with stories about recipes and ingredients. Customers order monthly boxes or one-off jars. Home-cook ships in courier-friendly packaging.

ideas.name: Festival Catering
ideas.for_whom: You, if you or your family cook well for groups
ideas.problem: Big caterers refuse functions below 50 people. Hosts of small birthdays, naming ceremonies, satsangs have nowhere to turn at 300-500/head.
ideas.what_app_does: Menus by function type at different per-head prices. Hosts pick, specify dietary needs, lock the date. Order arrives on WhatsApp.

ideas.name: Poultry Direct
ideas.for_whom: You, if your family raises poultry, or a relative does
ideas.problem: Urban families pay 80-120/dozen for 'free-range' eggs they cannot verify. The farmer sells to wholesalers for 30.
ideas.what_app_does: Shows the farm and the chickens. Customers subscribe to weekly or biweekly egg deliveries. Delivery on a fixed route the farmer plans weekly.

ideas.name: Senior Travel
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have an elderly relative who travels, or you like planning trips
ideas.problem: Indian seniors have travel budgets but SOTC and Thomas Cook treat them as generic tourists. Trusted senior-paced trips are hard to find.
ideas.what_app_does: Curated small-group senior tours (pilgrimage, heritage, hill stations). Travellers browse, book, pay. Founder handles all logistics end-to-end.
